nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; tends to inhabit coral-rich outer reef slopes and lagoon walls, grazing on algae and small invertebrates among the rockwork

decorPreferences: established reef with coralline algae growth

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on algae, small invertebrates, and detritus in the wild. In captivity, has been reported to accept a varied diet including Spirulina, marine algae, frozen mysis shrimp, high-quality angelfish preparations, and other meaty foods. Access to live rock for continuous grazing is generally considered beneficial.

avoidWith: Tridacna clams (risk of nipping at mantle)

summary: A yellow-bodied dwarf angelfish from the Southwestern Pacific, distinguished from the similar Centropyge heraldi by a black spot at the rear of the dorsal fin. Generally considered intermediate-level; best kept singly in a well-established reef or FOWLR aquarium with ample live rock.
careNotes:
Woodhead's Angelfish (Centropyge woodheadi) is named after Phil Woodhead, an underwater photographer from Cairns, Queensland. It is closely related to the Yellow Pygmy Angelfish (Centropyge heraldi), differing visually in the presence of a dark spot at the posterior end of the dorsal fin. The two species overlap in parts of the Western and Southwestern Pacific, but woodheadi's range is centered on the Southwestern Pacific (Great Barrier Reef to the Gambier archipelago), while heraldi is more broadly distributed across the Indo-Pacific.

This species is generally considered 'reef safe with caution': isolated cases of nipping at large-polyped stony corals and clam mantles have been reported. In a mature, well-fed reef tank its behavior tends to be acceptable, but the risk is generally considered non-trivial.

It has been known to be semi-aggressive toward conspecifics and other Centropyge dwarf angelfish; a single specimen per aquarium is generally advised unless the tank is large enough to provide distinct territories. Like most Centropyge species, it is believed to be a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first sex changer), and in the wild tends to form small harems of one dominant male with several females.

Ample hiding places and established live rock are generally considered important for acclimation and long-term health. Multiple small feedings per day of varied foods — Spirulina, marine algae, frozen mysis shrimp, and other meaty fare — are commonly recommended.

Tank-size source: LiveAquaria recommends a 70-gallon (265 L) minimum; 300 L was chosen per the playbook nice-values list as the nearest cautious increment, reflecting the species' semi-aggressive territorial nature and need for rockwork territory.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely documented for this species. Like other Centropyge angelfish, it is believed to be a protogynous hermaphrodite; no reliable external sex differentiation is known from the aquarium trade. In the wild it tends to form small harems, but replication of this in home aquariums has not been widely reported.

additional.name: centropyge.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity, diet, tank decor preferences, and native range. Confirms woodheadi vs heraldi distinction (black dorsal spot). Did not provide quantitative water parameters or tank-size data. Range given as western and central Pacific (Japan to Australia), broader than FishBase's Southwestern Pacific definition; FishBase range was preferred as more specific.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), native range (Southwestern Pacific: Great Barrier Reef to Gambier archipelago), and species identity. Confirmed harmless status and high resilience rating. Did not provide aquarium-specific parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved and considered very difficult. No reliable external sex differentiation is known; the species is believed to be protogynous. Harem formation occurs in the wild but has not been reliably replicated in home aquariums.
